one_line_takeaway: A broad early-2024 narrative survey of LLMs and multi-modal vision-language models that usefully taxonomizes how MM-LLMs bolt a vision encoder onto an LLM and catalogs fine-tuning methods, but it is a non-security literature review with several factual slips and no original evaluation.
key_finding: There is no single agreed-upon way to co-train a foundational text model with other modalities: the reviewed MM-LLMs differ fundamentally in how vision and language components are frozen/trained across the (typically two-stage) pipeline (e.g., MiniGPT4 freezes both LLM and vision model and trains only a projection layer; LLaVA freezes both in stage 1 then trains the LLM in stage 2; mPLUG-OWL trains vision then fine-tunes the LLM via LoRA; Kosmos-1/2 train the LLM alongside frozen vision models in a single combined stage using grounded image-text pairs). CLIP recurs as the near-universal visual backbone. The authors also emphasize that smaller, better-data-curated models can beat larger ones (LLaMA-13B > GPT-3 on most benchmarks; Falcon's RefinedWeb curation), and that open-source models bring transparency/auditability benefits weighed against support and licensing caveats.
core_contribution: A narrative survey (not an experimental paper) by Carolan, Fennelly and Smeaton (Dublin City University, arXiv:2404.01322, Mar 2024) reviewing the state of Large Language Models with a specific focus on multi-modal / vision LLMs (MM-LLMs). Its own contribution is the synthesis and comparative organization: it (1) recaps LLM history and attention variants (self-attention/MHA, MQA, GQA), (2) argues the open-source-vs-proprietary trade-off (cost, transparency, EU AI Act auditability, data control), (3) profiles the major text-origin LLMs (GPT family, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA/LLaMA-2, MedAlpaca, Mistral-7B, Falcon-7B/40B/180B, Grok-1) in a comparative Table 1, (4) profiles vision and MM-LLMs (BLIP-2, ViT, CLIP/RA-CLIP, LLaVA, Kosmos-1/2, MiniGPT4, mPLUG-OWL) and taxonomizes how each couples a vision encoder to an LLM (Table 2, Figure 2), (5) surveys model-tuning techniques (full fine-tuning, PEFT via LoRA/QLoRA/SFT, prompt engineering, RLHF), and (6) discusses hallucinations, ethics, and evaluation benchmarks. The distinguishing framing versus a prior LLM survey it cites ([12]) is the cross-modal (text/image/video/audio) scope and the open-source ethics lens.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a non-security survey paper; it does not define an adversary, attack surface, or defense. It touches security-adjacent themes only in passing and descriptively: model misuse, training-data bias (e.g., CLIP's disproportionate mis-classification of people of colour into non-human FairFace classes at 14% vs 8%), hallucinations as reliability failures, and open-source transparency aiding audits/EU AI Act compliance. No threat model is asserted by the authors and none is invented here.
limitations: As a survey it is descriptive rather than evaluative: it does not run head-to-head benchmarks itself, so all comparisons rely on heterogeneous vendor-reported and paper-reported numbers under differing conditions. Coverage is a snapshot (early 2024) and already dated. The text contains several factual/typographical errors that reduce reliability, e.g., Claude is dated 'first introduced as Claude 1 in March 2024' (Claude 1 was March 2023) while 'Claude 3 was introduced in 2023' (Claude 3 was March 2024); Grok-1 is described as 'developed by xAI and released by OpenAI' (xAI is not OpenAI); vendor names are misspelled ('Antrhophic'/'Antropic'); and 'RefinedWeb'/'SWA' are mistyped in places. Table 1 lists speculative/'Unknown' figures for closed models. No systematic methodology for paper selection is stated.

datasets_used: None constructed by the authors (survey). Datasets referenced from the reviewed works include: MS-COCO (LLaVA 158k language-vision instructions; MiniGPT4 caption benchmark)
The Pile and Common Crawl interleaved image-text data (Kosmos-1)
Common-sense/reasoning benchmarks cited: MMLU, HellaSwag, Winogrande, ARC, OpenBookQA, PIQA, BoolQ, HumanEval, TruthfulQA, RealToxicityPrompts, CrowS-Pairs, WinoGender
quantitative_results: GPT-4 scored in the ~90th percentile on the bar exam vs GPT-3.5 in the bottom 10%
Claude 3 context window = 200,000 tokens; knowledge cutoff Aug 2023 (as stated by the paper)
Gemini Ultra scored 64% on MMMU, >5 points above the prior best; Gemini context length = 32k tokens
LLaMA parameter range 7B-65B; LLaMA-13B reported to outperform GPT-3 on most benchmarks; LLaMA-65B competitive with Chinchilla and PaLM-540B; evaluated on 20 benchmarks
LLaMA-2 range 7B-70B; ~40% larger pre-training corpus and context doubled from 2048 to 4096 tokens vs LLaMA
MedAlpaca-13B zero-shot USMLE steps 1/2/3 accuracy = 47.3% / 47.7% / 60.2% (SFT), dropping to 25.0% / 25.5% / 25.5% with LoRA fine-tuning
Mistral-7B reported to outperform LLaMA-2 13B Chat
Falcon models trained on 1.5 trillion tokens; Falcon-180B on 3.5 trillion tokens; memory needs 320GB (180B) / 40GB (40B) / 15GB (7B); RefinedWeb 600B-token public extract
Grok-1 = 314B parameters, mixture of 8 experts; 63.2% on HumanEval, 73% on MMLU
CLIP FairFace bias: people of colour mis-classified into non-human classes at 14% vs 8%; RA-CLIP claims +12.7% zero-shot image-classification improvement over CLIP baseline
LLaVA fine-tuned on 158k MS-COCO language-vision instructions, 3 epochs, lr 2e-5, batch size 32

MiniGPT4 stage-1 trained on ~5M image-text pairs (~10 hours on 80GB A100); stage-2 refined on 3,500 high-quality pairs (~7 minutes on A100); 65% success on meme interpretation vs BLIP-2 <10%; >65% vs <30% (BLIP-2) on MS-COCO captioning
mPLUG-OWL evaluated on OwlEval = 82 questions over 50 images
QLoRA reported to enable fine-tuning a 65B model on a single 48GB GPU
baselines_compared: Not applicable - the paper runs no experiments of its own and therefore has no baselines. Comparisons it reports are between reviewed models (e.g., LLaMA-13B vs GPT-3; MiniGPT4 vs BLIP-2; Mistral-7B vs LLaMA-2 13B Chat; Grok-1 vs GPT-4/Claude 2), drawn from the original source papers rather than measured by the authors.
